title: 'Barbie' Botox can be dangerous! Should 'trap tox' be done for aesthetic purposes？
filename: _Barbie_ Botox can be dangerous! Should _trap tox_ be done for aesthetic purposes？.en.vtt
excerpt: Various members of my team are extremely angry about a new trend so I thought we would discuss Barbie Botox this idea that because and only discovered this about 10 seconds ago Barbie does not have a trapezius muscle and people want to look like Barbie so they are botoxing their …

segments.text: various members of my team are extremely angry about a new trend so I thought we would discuss Barbie Botox this idea that because and only discovered this about 10 seconds ago Barbie does not have a trapezius muscle and people want to look like Barbie so they are botoxing their trapezius muscles into Oblivion so that's what we're talking about in this week's show but let's start with considering what the perceived benefits of this type of procedure are I from what I can see this is named after the Barbie character because she actually has no trapezius if you look at the shape of her body which I'd never done in so much detail um she actually doesn't have any elevation at all there's a complete right angle like no human I've actually ever seen that it really is completely flat now I think it's associated with femininity to be a certain type of femininity it's not typical female shape that's for sure but it's a kind of Slender youthful look that people are trying to get by shrinking this muscle and a longer neck

is more elegant lots of people like that and one way is to shrink whatever is competing with the neck and so I think that's what people are after it's the sense of a long elegant neck by shrinking the muscles that actually support the head now the other interesting thing about this procedure is that it has its origins in medical interventions for things such as torticollis and also migraine injections so it's on the migraine protocol that you inject the trapezius so we have been injecting the muscle for a long time but what's changing is the indication it had been an indication if you had a lot of tension in the muscle or if you had a migraine that's triggered by neck pain to use botuline toxins but now it's becoming an indication just because you want the muscle to look smaller now it's not a new idea to shrink a muscle with botulinum toxin obviously we do it with Master muscles often with patients with bruxism and we also do it in calves so this is the idea that a thinner more slender lower leg is more attractive and so people actually shrink their carve muscles with botulinum toxin also something that I've never quite understood it I don't find a very

narrow tapering lower leg any more attractive than someone with a curvature to it but these are one of the ideas that seem to be influenced by different cultures so Dr subio also posted a thought-provoking and amusing cartoon where he stated that nothing sticks it to the patriarchy quite like chemically elongating your neck so I like this because obviously the Barbie movie is very much about sticking into the patriarchy but what we've ended up doing is using the attention that Barbie's getting to sell more treatments to make women feel a little bit less comfortable in their skin because now they have a problem with a trapezius muscle that they didn't know existed before and this is always the conflict we have I think in Medical Aesthetics which is that we want to empower people to feel better but it's quite often an accident along the way if you think of it as an accident maybe it's not an accident that we first make them feel worse about something that they'd never noticed before we're essentially creating a new way to compete that wasn't there before with each other which is about who's got the smallest

trapezius muscle or the longest neck and that was not a thing before the Barbie movie so it is it's ironic and that's exactly what this cartoon depicts so well of course the biggest downside I think is that in order to get a bigger difference you will have to create a weaker muscle and it's exactly where the muscle needs to be the strongest if you think about the forces that are imposed upon your head and your neck the point with maximum leverage on your neck is right where you're causing atrophy so that would be my concern and I don't I know we don't have any evidence I think that is likely to cause a problem it does make intuitive sense that there will be a small number of people who will have a negative outcome from this now I've noticed this even when treating platysmal bands that there are patients who notice that their head feels heavier and that it's harder work doing things so for example sitting up straight if you do a sit-up you will often see people use their platysmal muscle as well so it's probably five percent of the total strength required to lift your head but patients notice that now that's

going to be even more of an issue when you're treating the actual one of the biggest muscles that supports the neck if there's one thing I've learned over the last 15 16 years in Medical Aesthetics is don't be the first to rush in when new things are announced as possible I've now seen this in fact even this year there are three different complications that have emerged from new procedures that often enter the market with a big Fanfare and everyone's talking about it and then you discover six months nine months down the line that they're causing a lot of misery for usually a small number of those patients but it's never talked about until it's already happening and that's because people don't want to be left behind and I would advise that you become quite comfortable with being left behind with new untested procedures and just observe what happens learn from it and then if you decide it's the right thing to do you can incorporate it I also want to discuss a reaction that Layla my producer had to this new procedure so that we can also look at it from a different perspective so what I thought would be fun is we'll include at the end of the show three different images and I

want to know which one you think looks the most beautiful so it's Layla some one of them's Photoshop one of them isn't um but I want you to tell us what you think is the most beautiful so Leila tell me what you first thought I think this is less mainly because you get BOTOX in your face but one thing that you always say and lots of good injections say is you should never aim for completely Frozen because then you're inhibiting your ability to effectively communicate to others for me this is kind of the same thing when you're injecting your trapezius so that you cause muscle atrophy then you are taking away your requirement for basic movement you know lifting things up carrying things you're yeah you're lessening the actual strength you have in this area and you think about you use this for loads of things why would you want to be weak why would you want to be weaker just so that you can have a longer neck which really doesn't mean that much anyway I

do think there's something elegant about a longer neck but I don't know if the shape you're going to get is more beautiful like I think there's something around if you actually look at the Barbie doll now that I've spotted it there's something missing it's not yeah it doesn't look right to me to have this right angle at your neck and I think maybe one of the things would be you could just reframe what beauty is as having a trapezius and then people can go to the gym and work out and get some trap definition yeah and just see that as beautiful yeah and in fact up big drive to you know muscly women strong women and until this Barbie movie came out I kind of think in a certain bubble of people the trend was to look muscly and to look strong also Barbie is for years she's been deemed as promoting unrealistic beauty standards and surely if there's anything more unrealistic it's having an alien length long neck with no trapezius muscle so have we not learned from that yeah I mean Barbie is

a caricature really um if you actually had a real person who looked like Barbie you would scream and leave run out of the building yeah I think they actually did a study on that and they made Barbie look like a real human it looked absolutely ridiculous like an alien the only thing I would say is you just need to have a do a proper medical consultation and guide people and make them aware of both the pros and the cons there will be people who probably have something aesthetic that is holding them back that you could improve with this procedure um but I don't think we should be aiming for zero trap being like a Barbie doll um being the being this that the new aesthetic goal I don't think there's any basis for that if you want to learn more about how treatments should be built into your patients lives to actually make them happier and not just make them look different and keep them healthier I've done a whole video called how to make your patients happier it's one of my favorite videos I've done and I'd like you to watch it so click on that
